data.title: West is getting on the same page as China for children’s social media use
data.description:
China’s National Press and Publication Administration introduced gaming regulations for under 18s in 2019, including a curfew and daily playing time limits of 90 minutes on weekdays and three hours at weekends and on public holidays. When the authorities tightened the rules in 2021, some Western media characterised the measures as draconian and a textbook example of state overreach.
Since then, the regulation of minors’ online activity has attracted wider attention as more governments grapple with the effects of social media.
Australia has become the first Western democracy to legally ban under 16s from using certain social media platforms, including X, Facebook, Instagram, YouTube and Snapchat. Under the new regulations, technology companies could face penalties for noncompliance. With this move, Canberra is signalling that it now considers access to social media to be a matter of public health and safety.
Europe and several Asian governments are considering similar measures. Singapore’s Ministry of Education has focused on schools. Starting in January, secondary school students will no longer be allowed to use smartphones or smartwatches during school hours, consistent with policies already in place in primary schools. The aim is to reset the school day as a time for face-to-face interaction and focused learning.
Advocates worldwide warn that unrestricted smartphone use during the school day hampers both learning and well-being. Teachers report that personal devices distract students from lessons, with notifications and apps encouraging constant task switching rather than engagement.
Research studies link heavy social media use among adolescents to elevated risks of cyberbullying, social comparison and disrupted sleep patterns. These factors are associated with higher rates of anxiety, depression and, in some cases, suicidal thoughts and self-harm.

Schools in countries such as France, Spain and Britain have implemented phone restrictions on the grounds of child protection rather than discipline.
A recent Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development report on childhood in the digital age found that while devices offer the benefits of learning, play and social connection, intensive and poorly managed use can affect children’s development, sleep, physical health and mental well-being, with links to anxiety, depression and problematic online experiences.
With several governments closely monitoring the developments in Australia, we are witnessing a shift in the social contract between the state, parents and children. Ideologies may differ between Beijing and Brussels, or between Asian and Western capitals, but they face the same fundamental dilemma: how to let young people grow up in a connected world without leaving them vulnerable to products never designed with their well-being in mind.
Many parents’ worries are not entirely new, but technology has intensified and extended them. Traditional teasing and playground gossip now follow children home through their phones, turning what was once time-limited conflict into 24-hour digital confrontation that can be screenshotted, shared and replayed.
International studies recognise cyberbullying as a significant public health issue: young people targeted online are more likely to report symptoms of depression and anxiety, and feelings of loneliness and exclusion.
The same pattern holds for broader social media use. Being left out, judged by peers or exposed to unrealistic standards has always been part of growing up. Constant connectivity amplifies these pressures. Many children rely on online spaces for friendship, self-expression and support. These same spaces can create anxiety, disrupt rest and magnify unkindness in ways that are difficult to manage.


The perspectives of young people cannot be ignored. For the current generation, social media platforms are more than just apps to pass the time; they are the modern equivalent of the schoolyard, art studio and playground combined.
Many young people devote considerable time and effort to building online communities and regard these networks as essential to their well-being and future careers. For them, being banned from a social media platform feels less like protection and more like eviction from a space they helped to create – one they are likely to try to access again.
Children spend more of their connected time at home than in class, and research on problematic social media use and internet overuse consistently points to the quality of family relationships and communication as a significant factor in both the causation and alleviation of psychological distress.
When parents set reasonable screen time limits, model their own phone habits and make time to talk about what children are seeing and doing online, it can reduce some of the risks associated with social media use and help young people cope better with upsetting content or peer pressure.
For any internet or social media policy to be effective, measures at home and at school must work together rather than operate separately. Guidance from education and child protection authorities increasingly emphasises that online safety is a shared responsibility: schools can establish clear rules, use filtered networks and teach digital citizenship, while parents can supervise daily activities, offer emotional support and foster an environment conducive to open and honest conversations when issues arise.
In that sense, combining age limits, school restrictions, stronger platform regulation and practical support for families offers a shared approach whereby governments from very different political traditions, whether in Asia or the West, accept that they face a common problem. Families, educators and regulators all have a part to play in helping children navigate an online world that is not going away.

data.title: China’s new quantum computer hits stability milestone, beating Google on efficiency
data.description:
Chinese researchers have taken a major step in the global race to build practical quantum computers, becoming the first team outside the United States – and the second in the world after Google – to cross a key threshold that determines whether these machines can work reliably at scale.
A team led by Pan Jianwei at the University of Science and Technology of China said their superconducting quantum computer, Zuchongzhi 3.2, had reached the fault-tolerant threshold – a point where fixing errors made the system more stable rather than less, overcoming a long-standing problem in which the very process of error correction introduces new mistakes.
Their research, published last week in the journal Physical Review Letters, relied on microwave-based control rather than the hardware-intensive error-suppression methods used by Google. The Chinese approach “could offer a more efficient route than Google’s” to building large, fault-tolerant quantum computers, the team said in a statement on Monday.
Joseph Emerson, a physicist at the University of Waterloo in Canada who was not involved in the research, said the study tackled one of quantum computing’s most difficult problems: qubits drifting out of their intended states and quietly spreading errors through the system.
Writing in the American Physical Society’s Physics magazine, Emerson described the experiment as “an impressive feat”, while cautioning that it remained far from the scale needed for practical, real-world applications.


Quantum computers work by harnessing the laws of quantum physics rather than the simple on-off logic used by ordinary computers. In theory, this allows them to tackle certain tasks – such as optimising complex systems or simulating molecules – in minutes that would take today’s machines thousands of years to complete.
In practice, however, quantum computers face a fundamental obstacle: instability. Their building blocks, known as qubits, are extremely sensitive to heat, noise and tiny disturbances from their surroundings, causing errors to appear constantly during normal operation.
To manage this, scientists developed quantum error correction, which spreads information across many qubits and repeatedly checks for problems. But this creates a paradox: each extra qubit and each additional check also introduces new sources of error.
For years, attempts to correct mistakes made systems less reliable, not more.
That is why researchers focused on a critical tipping point known as the error-correction threshold. Below this threshold, error correction backfires and creates more mistakes than it removes. Above it, the balance flips and error correction delivers a net benefit, allowing systems to become more stable as they grow.
China and the US both began investing early in surface code quantum error correction, one of the most widely studied methods for protecting quantum information. In 2022, Pan’s team used an earlier processor, Zuchongzhi 2, to achieve a minimal error-correcting unit, known as a distance-3 surface code logical qubit, as an initial proof of principle.
The following year, Google pushed the technique further by achieving distance-5 surface code error correction. But in both cases, relatively high error rates in the underlying qubits prevented the systems from truly crossing the threshold.
That changed in February, when Google reported a breakthrough using its Willow quantum processor. By suppressing a particularly harmful class of errors known as leakage using direct current pulses, Google became the world’s first team to achieve a distance-7 surface code logical qubit operating below the threshold.
This approach, however, places tight constraints on chip design and requires increasingly complex wiring in ultra-low-temperature environments as systems scale up, according to Pan’s team.
In the new study, the Chinese researchers took a different path. Working with the 107-qubit Zuchongzhi 3.2 processor, they developed an all-microwave method to suppress leakage errors, using carefully timed microwave signals instead of additional hardware controls.
Combining this approach with surface code error correction, Pan and coworkers were able to build a distance-7 logical qubit, matching the scale of Google’s most advanced demonstrations. They found that as the system grew larger, the overall error rate fell rather than rose.
The researchers measured an error-suppression factor of 1.4, meaning each increase in the size of the error-correction code reduced errors instead of amplifying them, which was clear evidence that the system was operating below the threshold.
They said that the all-microwave approach could offer practical advantages as quantum computers grow larger. Because microwave signals can be multiplexed, allowing multiple signals to travel along a single wire, the method might reduce wiring complexity and hardware overhead, two major obstacles to scaling quantum processors.
Taken together, they said, the results pointed to a more flexible and potentially more scalable route towards fault-tolerant quantum computers with hundreds of thousands or even millions of qubits.

data.title: ‘Can’t buy true love’: Chinese man abducted as child returns to birth family after reunion
data.description:
A man in China who was abducted at the age of four has reunited with his birth parents and severed ties with the family who brought him up.
On December 12, Peng Congcong, now 26, originally from Jiangxi province in southeastern China, shared an online post looking back at his first year after reuniting with his birth family.
Peng said his family moved to Beijing when he was four and he was abducted after being lured away while playing alone near a market.
His devastated parents reported him missing, put up posters and searched for him for 21 years.

During that time, Peng was raised by a family in Jiangsu province in eastern China and renamed “Zhang Kun”.
Some mainland media reports have described that family as the “buyer”, though Peng has not publicly responded to the claim.
Last December, police told Peng he was not from Jiangsu but from Jiangxi and said his birth family had located him via DNA testing.
He then travelled to Beijing to reunite with his parents and two older sisters.
Peng said they took him back to the market where he had lived as a child and showed him local sights.
Days later, they returned to Jiangxi, where villagers welcomed him with fireworks and a banquet. Relatives also prepared red envelopes and a cake to celebrate his return.

Peng wrote: “This is what a real home feels like. With my parents’ care, my sisters by my side and my relatives’ blessings, I feel a sense of belonging deep inside me.”
At the time, Peng’s job and friends were in Jiangsu, where he also had a house and a car.
After reuniting with his birth family, he quit his job, changed his household registration and sold the home and car.
“These things do not belong to me. So I returned them,” he said.
In a live-stream, Peng explained that his adoptive parents had bought the home, while he had paid for the renovations.
He cut off all ties in Jiangsu and moved back to Jiangxi to start again.
In the post, Peng described 2025 as his “rebirth year”, shaped by love and reunion.
He wrote: “For the past 20 years, my parents lived with guilt and pain. Now I take them travelling and try good food with them. I want to make up, bit by bit, for the time the years stole from us.”
Peng also became a volunteer helping families search for missing children and earned money by selling household goods on social media.

He donated all the proceeds from his first live-stream to the missing children charity Baby Come Home.
China has long grappled with human trafficking, with cases tied to illegal adoption as well as sexual and labour exploitation.
Official data shows that cases involving the abduction and trafficking of women and children in 2021 were down 86.2 per cent from 2013, but many children taken years ago are still missing.
Peng’s story struck a chord on mainland social media.
One online observer said: “I admire his courage. Not everyone can give up everything they have built and start over in a new city. I hope his family finds happiness.”
“Traffickers and buyers should both face tough punishment. Money cannot buy true love or family,” said another.

data.title: Quiet strength: how Hong Kong’s Tai Po community is moving on 1 month after fire
data.description:
Tai Po residents are forging ahead with new paths forward following a catastrophic blaze that claimed the lives of 161 people. In this series, the Post looks into how the community is moving on and rebuilding their lives.
A heavy quiet has settled over the neighbourhood in Hong Kong’s Tai Po where seven towers stand charred.
Gone are the piles of flowers and notes bearing messages of grief and condolences that filled a makeshift memorial site at the Kwong Fuk Sitting-out Area, where mourners once waited in zigzagging lines.
Only a small basket of white and yellow bouquets remains across the road from the fire-ravaged Wang Fuk Court housing estate.
The neighbourhood is hushed, but residents said the silence itself bore witness to what the deadly blaze took from their once-vibrant community.
“It used to be a beautiful, lively neighbourhood, with constant chatter from elderly residents, young people and pupils, but it is quiet now and feels almost lifeless,” said Cheung Siu-fan, a four-decade resident of nearby Kwong Fuk Estate.
“I previously enjoyed the view of the Wang Fuk Court towers illuminated at night – they looked magnificent,” recalled the 70-year-old, whose flat on the 27th floor of Kwong Shung House now overlooks the blackened buildings.
“Now, the view fills me with dread.”

One month on, residents of the Tai Po community are still picking up the pieces after the catastrophic blaze that broke out at Wang Fuk Court in the afternoon of November 26.
The fire quickly spread, escalating to the maximum “five-alarm” emergency and consuming seven of the eight 31-storey towers covered in bamboo scaffolding and green mesh netting for renovation works.
The city watched in shock as the deadliest inferno in decades engulfed the buildings while firefighters battled to rescue trapped residents. The fire, which raged for 43 hours, killed 161 people, including a firefighter, and displaced nearly 5,000 residents.
In the aftermath of the tragedy, thousands of mourners poured into the neighbourhood to pay their respects to the victims, while volunteers and support groups mobilised to provide emergency supplies as well as mental health counselling for those affected.
Tranquillity seemed to have returned as the community struggled to heal and move on. Yet even the slightest mention of the disaster or a mere glimpse of the estate can bring tears to the eyes of those who have lost friends and relatives.
Cheung’s voice cracked as she recalled losing two old friends – a couple in their seventies – in the fire. Six other friends had been relocated to Yuen Long and Sheung Shui, she said.
“I used to play mahjong with them and meet for Sunday yum cha, but all that is gone,” said the grandmother who has lived with her husband in their public housing flat since 1985.
She said she had watched in horror from her flat as the towers were engulfed in flames, and the images had stayed with her. She has trouble sleeping, and the sound of fire alarms terrifies her now.
“I wonder when this trauma will fade,” she said.
A 69-year-old housewife, who only gave her surname as Ko and has lived in Kwong Fuk Estate for about 20 years, said she made a 10-minute detour from her usual route to avoid seeing Wang Fuk Court whenever she went shopping, despite discomfort in her legs.
“I’m still not ready to face the sight of the burned estate,” said Ko, who stayed at her son’s home for three days after the fire broke out.
Like many other residents, especially the elderly, Ko lamented the inconvenience caused by ongoing road closures and the suspension of several bus routes in and out of the neighbourhood.
With the absence of nearly 2,000 households from Wang Fuk Court, the nearby Kwong Fuk Commercial Centre and Kwong Fuk Market have lost much of their vibrancy, with drastically reduced foot traffic and sluggish business. A bakery closed within days of the fire.
Around noon, most of the restaurants there, from big chains to smaller eateries, were half-empty.

Wong Miu-yu, owner of a decade-old cart noodle shop, said the number of patrons had dropped by half since the fire. Two regular customers were among those killed, she added.
“We used to see diners gradually coming in during dinner hours, but now we serve fewer than 10 patrons a night,” she said, noting that the community now had little appetite for dining out.
Still, Wong said she was heartened by the support of about a dozen Wang Fuk Court residents who returned to her restaurant.
Next to Wong’s restaurant is a laundry shop which Alice Ng has run for 12 years. In the days following the blaze, she made more than 20 calls to her regular customers from the estate to check on them and found that four elderly patrons were among the deceased, she said.
Ng, who also lives in Tai Po, said her business plunged by about 60 per cent. Along with several other shop owners there, she was negotiating with the landlord for a rent cut.
She added that she might close the shop when her lease expired next year despite her long ties to the neighbourhood.
Chan Chi-man also said he would consider shutting down his 10-year-old fish stall in the neighbourhood wet market after business halved following the fire. He already let go of an employee to cut costs and now works alone.
“Business has been difficult, even worse than during the Covid-19 pandemic,” he said. “I’ll consider moving elsewhere after my lease expires in a year. I can’t bear to leave here but I still have to make a living.”
Though the sombre atmosphere lingers, glimmers of warmth and generosity bring comfort and hope.
A dim sum shop is offering complimentary meals to Wang Fuk Court residents in the aftermath of the tragedy.
The Kenford Chinese Medicine Clinic in Kwong Fuk Commercial Centre has also provided free medical services to affected residents.
Chinese medicine practitioner Chan Ching-hei said many had sought help over the past month for sleeping problems, poor mental health and emotional distress.
“The lack of a comfortable, familiar place to rest has exacerbated their stress,” he said.
Despite the devastation to the estate, some Wang Fuk Court residents continued to return, clinging on to hope that their homes and community would one day be rebuilt.
Housewife Hung Choi-ying, 59, who had lived in the estate’s Wang Tao House, regularly returned to the neighbourhood with her husband from their temporary flat. The couple met friends and visited shops they had patronised for years.
“We have an unbreakable bond with this place,” she said.
Hung added that she hoped the lost homes could be rebuilt and the neighbourhood would be restored.
As the sun set and night fell, the street lights flickered on, and the nearby homes glowed softly, standing in stark contrast to the burned high-rises shrouded in darkness.
Every now and then, passers-by on the footbridge to the neighbourhood stopped to photograph the charred towers.
Across the road, cyclists streamed along the trail. A few paused to gaze up at the estate and read the heart-shaped stickers arranged into three wreaths on the roadside wire mesh. Each carried handwritten messages of support for Wang Fuk Court residents, beside wilted bouquets left by mourners days ago.
Businessman Roy Wai stood alone in the Kwong Fuk Sitting-out Area, gazing at the charred towers.
The 48-year-old had returned to Hong Kong from work in Canada two weeks earlier. He said that after watching the news abroad, he felt he had to visit the site and pay his respects to the victims.
“I will never forget the tragedy and the lives lost in it,” he said.
Additional reporting by Susan Su and Themis Chen

data.title: Lacrosse boss upbeat about sport as ‘legendary’ Japanese coach makes Hong Kong debut
data.description:
Hong Kong’s lacrosse chief has said he was optimistic about the team’s prospects after bringing in a coach who could take the development of the sport to the “next level”.
Well-respected Japanese coach Takeshi Sato became head coach of Hong Kong’s men’s and women’s teams in May after working as a director of the High Performance Training Department of the Japan Lacrosse Association.
He will make his coaching debut for the city’s teams at the upcoming Hong Kong International Lacrosse Sixes, which runs from Saturday to Monday at the Hong Kong Football Club.
“I knew Sato in person for years; he’s the coach of those who coach the national team in Japan, so he’s reputable,” said Raymond Fong Kong, president of the Hong Kong, China Lacrosse Association.
“Our former women’s team coach Yoko Shibata was also his pupil and she’s now been called back to Japan for the preparation towards the Los Angeles Games in 2028.”
Fong, who flew to Okinawa in March last year to interview the “legendary” Sato, said it took months to put the finishing touches on the appointment.


“I talked to him all night and it was not easy to bring him in because he’s like a Hall of Famer, a legend, so the Japanese Lacrosse Association had to give approval,” he said.
“Sato is also the head coach of a few universities, so he can be with us for only 10 days every month.”
Fong added that Sato brought new insights and he was “coaching at the macro level” to provide strategies to instruct his coaching team.
“We always focused on hard-core training such as technique and stamina,” he said. “But he saw the soft-core aspects of psychological quality and stress resistance, players’ bonding and team chemistry.
“We didn’t pay much attention to those, and looking back, those were the reasons we haven’t played so well in some matches over the past period.”
Fong also said the recruitment of Sato was like “performing a health check” on the city’s lacrosse development.
“He’ll give us instructions and those surrounding the sport, helping us to think out of the box, and we’ll follow it through,” he said. “There’s no change in the direction of development but something at the next level.
“It’s beyond the techniques; it’s about a deeper understanding of how to perform on the field and handle world-class competitions.
“I hope Sato will remain with us for the next couple of years. I’m afraid there won’t be too much time for us, given the next Olympics in Los Angeles. The Japanese association will surely want to bring him back eventually.”
Sato, who has worked in lacrosse for 23 years, has admired Hong Kong’s lacrosse team since the Asia-Pacific Championships in Bangkok a decade ago.
“That’s the first time I witnessed Hong Kong’s lacrosse,” said the 49-year-old, who began his coaching career in 2002. “The passion and strong desire for improvement that the players showed at that time left a very positive and lasting impression on me.
“I have witnessed the remarkable development of Hong Kong lacrosse over the past decade, and I strongly felt I wanted to be a contributor to that progress … The emotion and conviction I felt 10 years ago were absolutely right, and that feeling grows even stronger every day.”

data.title: Is North Korea’s new nuclear sub reveal a bid to counter US-South Korean naval power?
data.description:
North Korea’s move to publicly reveal the full hull and displacement of its first nuclear-powered submarine under construction appears to be a strategic counter to bids by the US and South Korea to expand their undersea capabilities.
North Korea’s state media on Thursday released images of leader Kim Jong-un inspecting a “nuclear-powered strategic guided missile submarine” being built at an undisclosed location. Kim was accompanied by his wife Ri Sol-ju and daughter Kim Ju-ae, believed to be groomed as a possible successor.
The report also revealed for the first time that the vessel’s displacement was 8,700 tonnes (9,600 tons), putting it in the same league as some American fast attack submarines.
In photos accompanying project reports in March, only the lower portion of the hull was shown.

“By publicising [its] progress in the construction of a nuclear-powered submarine, North Korea is responding to the South’s move to build a fleet of nuclear-powered submarines,” Hong Min, director of the Korea Institute for National Unification’s North Korean Research Division, told This Week in Asia.
Pyongyang’s report came a day after South Korean national security adviser Wi Sung-lac said Seoul and Washington were starting working-level talks on the construction of nuclear-powered submarines early next year.
After returning from a week-long trip to the US, Canada and Japan, Wi said the two allies held “practical and in-depth consultations” to implement a joint fact sheet released last month following President Lee Jae-myung’s meeting with US President Donald Trump on the sidelines of the Apec summit.
As the new images showed a completely assembled hull, this suggested that a nuclear engine was already in place, with fissile fuel testing likely to be the next stage, Hong said.
The new submarine appeared to be an SSGN, or nuclear-powered guided-missile submarine armed with nuclear-capable cruise missiles for land attack and special operations, he said.

“However, we can’t rule out the possibility that the vessel would also carry a small number of submarine-launched ballistic missiles”, or torpedoes fitted with nuclear warheads.
North Korea has been testing various missiles, including nuclear-capable intercontinental ballistic missiles whose range could reportedly cover the United States.
‘Message of displeasure’
“The North is also sending a message of displeasure at the movement of US strategic assets, including the nuclear-powered attack submarine USS Greeneville,” Hong said.
The US vessel docked at the Busan Naval Operations Base on Tuesday to load military supplies and allow crew rest, Seoul said, adding that the port visit was intended to strengthen exchanges and cooperation between the two allies and reinforce their combined defence posture.
The last time a US nuclear-powered submarine docked in South Korea was when the USS Alexandria arrived in Busan in February.
At the inspection, Kim warned that South Korea’s plan to build nuclear-powered submarines would “worsen the instability” in the region.


“The DPRK regards it as an offensive act severely violating its security and maritime sovereignty,” Kim was quoted as saying by the state-run Korean Central News Agency (KCNA) using the acronym for North Korea’s official name, the Democratic People’s Republic of Korea.
While Kim was “probably right” that a build-up of nuclear-powered submarines would affect the stability around the Korean peninsula, he had only himself to blame, said Leif-Eric Easley, a political-science professor at Ewha Womans University in Seoul.
“It is Pyongyang that disavows diplomacy with Seoul, threatens its neighbours with nuclear weapons, and deepens the suffering of its own people by devoting massive resources to military dictatorship rather than economic development,” Easley said.
“Kim may realise that he has miscalculated once he observes South Korea’s superior technology, if North Korea experiences a major naval accident, or should largesse from Russia and China dry up before his ambitious projects are completed.”
The North Korean leader also said South Korea’s actions represented “a threat to its security that must be countered”, reaffirming that his country would “maintain its national security policy and principle of deterring the enemy”.
“It is needed to make the enemy understand with no doubt that they will be surely forced to pay a dear price when they violate the security of the DPRK’s strategic sovereignty and that they will face a merciless retaliatory attack if they try to select a military option,” he said.
Calling the new nuclear submarine “an epoch-making crucial change … [in] the war deterrent level”, he said the vessels under construction would “make a leap forward in bolstering up the combat capabilities of our fleets”.
He also promised to “increase and expand the speed and scale of building various surface and underwater warships and continuously equip them with different offensive weapon systems”.
These were all aimed at “permanently ensuring the peaceful environment and absolute security of the country”, he said.

Hong said the KCNA reports indicated North Korea was seeking to shift from “limited deterrence to maximum deterrence”, keeping open the option of pre-emptive nuclear strikes alongside assured retaliation.
Kim’s remarks show he was confident that nuclear submarines can compensate for vulnerabilities in the North’s land-based nuclear forces, which are exposed to US and South Korean precision strikes and superior air power, according to Hong.
His tone, however, was softer compared with past statements, minimising overtly hostile language and instead emphasising defence and security needs, Hong said.
In a separate report on Thursday, KCNA said that Kim had overseen the test-firing of new long-range anti-air missiles in the East Sea.
“The launched missiles accurately hit the mock targets at an altitude of 200km (124 miles),” KCNA said, calling the test “part of the routine activities”.
Yang Moo-jin, a professor at the University of North Korean Studies, said Kim’s remarks suggested that a halt to US-South Korea joint military drills and withdrawal of US strategic assets from the peninsula would remain prerequisites for any dialogue.
“The North is reaffirming indirectly its stance that its denuclearisation is out of the question under any circumstances,” Yang said.

data.title: US launches strikes against Isis in Nigeria, Trump says
data.description:
The United States ‌carried out air strikes against ‍Islamic State militants in northwest Nigeria, US President Donald Trump ⁠said on Thursday, claiming the group had been targeting Christians in ‍the region.
“Tonight, at my ‍direction as Commander in Chief, the ‌United States launched a powerful and ‍deadly strike against ISIS Terrorist Scum in Northwest Nigeria, who ‌have been ⁠targeting and viciously killing, primarily, innocent Christians, at levels not seen for ‌many years, and even Centuries!,” Trump said ‌in ‌a post on social media.
More to follow …

data.title: How The Joy Luck Club paved the way for Asian representation long before Crazy Rich Asians
data.description:
This is the latest instalment in a feature series reflecting on instances of East meets West in world cinema, including China-US co-productions.
When Wayne Wang’s The Joy Luck Club was released in 1993, it stood as only the second Hollywood film ever to tell a contemporary story with a majority Asian cast – the first, Flower Drum Song, had arrived 32 years prior.
Based on the bestselling 1989 novel by Amy Tan, it tells of four Chinese mothers and their daughters in San Francisco. They play a regular mahjong game, calling themselves The Joy Luck Club.
After the death of founding member Suyuan (Kieu Chinh), her daughter June (Ming-Na Wen) is invited to take her place.
“They were worried,” June says of her aunties. “In me, they see their own daughters: just as ignorant of all the hopes and dreams our mothers brought to this country.”


What follows is a complex tale of intergenerational trauma, in which we witness each mother’s struggles growing up in China and the subsequent impact on their American offspring.
Despite the success of the novel, it was a difficult sell.
“Nobody wanted to make the movie,” Wang recalled, “because everybody said, ‘Well, this is a movie about eight Chinese women, who’s going to go see it? You can’t even find enough good actresses to play them!’”
After securing a modest budget, he and Tan worked with screenwriter Ron Bass (Rain Man), who suggested voice-overs to help clarify the story.
“A lot of executives and producers are afraid of voice-overs because they say it distances the audience from the action,” said Bass. “I felt differently. It allowed you into the inner heart of the narrator and to understand their feelings in a way you could never do in dialogue.”

Bass also added the opening farewell party, which helps introduce the characters. It is being held in honour of June, who is travelling to China to meet the twin sisters her mother was forced to abandon as a young woman.
This narrative thread weaves into her own, which in turn interconnects with the next. Despite moments of melodrama, it is an elegant film, sensitively directed.
Wang shows a keen eye for group dynamics. At the party, the camera glides through the guests, capturing the older women arguing over food, the men watching sport and the fractious politics of a group photograph.
There are moments of desolate beauty too, such as when 15-year-old Lindo (Irene Ng) is handed over to her new husband’s family among the spindly winter trees.

The mothers’ lives in China are defined by brutality – marked by abuse, sexual assault, neglect and even infanticide – leaving wounds that seem too deep to heal.
After the death of her firstborn baby, Ying-Ying (France Nuyen) finds she has nothing but sorrow to pass on to her daughter, Lena (Lauren Tom), who “had no spirit because I had none to give her”.
An-Mei (Lisa Lu), meanwhile, sees her own mother sacrifice everything to become a rich man’s wife, only to watch her own daughter, Rose (Rosalind Chao), follow the same path.
Life is far from easy for their children. Both June and Waverly (Tamlyn Tomita) are burdened by the weight of their parents’ expectations.
At school, the young June (Melanie Chang) refuses to play piano because “this isn’t China, you can’t make me!” Young Waverly (Mai Vu) quits playing chess for similar reasons: “Why do you have to use me to show off?” she asks her mother.

Critics have complained about the film’s depiction of Asian men as cruel and abusive, yet this arguably overlooks the broader theme. Whether Chinese or American, mother, daughter or wife, each woman is a victim of the patriarchy, a system designed to oppress them.
“I was raised the Chinese way,” says An-Mei. “I was taught to desire nothing, to swallow other people’s misery, to eat my own bitterness. And even though I taught my daughter the opposite, she still came out the same way.
“Maybe it is because she was born to me and she was born a girl. And I was born to my mother and I was born a girl. All of us are like stairs, one step after another, going up and down, but all going the same way.”
By the film’s end, the mothers and daughters have, at least, come to understand each other better.
“I see you,” Suyuan tells June in flashback – three words that signal the beginning of healing.

What audiences saw was another matter. The Joy Luck Club made a respectable US$32.9 million at the box office and was selected for preservation in the US National Film Registry for being “culturally, historically or aesthetically significant” in 2020.
What it did not do was change things for the next generation. It would be 25 years before Hollywood took a chance on another contemporary tale with a majority Asian cast: 2018’s Crazy Rich Asians.
Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook

data.title: King Charles calls for ‘reconciliation’ in Christmas speech
data.description:
Britain’s King Charles called for “compassion and reconciliation” at a time of “division” across the world in his Christmas Day message on Thursday.
The 77-year-old monarch said he found it “enormously encouraging” how people of different faiths had a “shared longing for peace”.
Charles praised individuals who risked their lives to save others in situations of violence, including those caught up in the killings at a Jewish event at Bondi Beach in Australia this month.
“Individuals and communities have displayed spontaneous bravery, instinctively placing themselves in harm’s way to defend others,” said the king whose words were accompanied by images of events at Bondi.
Eighty years after the end of World War II, the king said the courage of servicemen and women and the way communities came together after the conflict carried “a timeless message for us all”.


“These are the values which have shaped our country,” he said.
“As we hear of division both at home and abroad, they are the values of which we must never lose sight,” Charles said in a message recorded at Westminster Abbey and broadcast nationally.
“With the great diversity of our communities, we can find the strength to ensure that right triumphs over wrong. It seems to me that we need to cherish the values of compassion and reconciliation the way our Lord lived and died.”
In October, Charles became the first head of the Church of England to pray publicly with a pope since a schism with Rome 500 years ago, in a service led by Pope Leo at the Vatican. He called it a “historic moment of spiritual unity”.
A few days earlier Charles met survivors of a deadly attack on a synagogue and members of the Jewish community in the northern English city of Manchester.

This is the second year running that the king has made his festive address away from a royal residence.
Last year he spoke from a former hospital chapel as he thanked medical staff for supporting the royal family in a year in which he announced his cancer diagnosis.
This year, Charles did not make any reference to his battle with the illness, nor did he mention his younger brother Andrew who in October was stripped of his royal titles over his links to convicted US sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
The message did feature images of his son and heir Prince William, as well as his grandson George.
The Christmas song that ended the message was sung by a Ukrainian choir formed after Russia’s 2022 invasion of their country.
The king and other members of the royal family, including Andrew’s daughters, attended a Christmas service at St Mary Magdalene Church on Charles’s private Sandringham estate in Norfolk.

data.title: British designer Faye Toogood on her humanist, playful homeware, interiors and clothing – interview
data.description:
Faye Toogood had worked at London-based design bible The World of Interiors for eight years when she decided to leave the two-dimensional world of glossy magazines behind in favour of things she could grasp and shape. From her kitchen table, she sowed the seeds for a multidisciplinary design studio that, alongside interior design, played with food, fragrance, sound and light – an approach that is de rigueur now in our age of food stylists and multi-hyphenate creative directors, but which set her apart in 2008.

“You were either a graphic designer, a furniture designer or a fashion designer,” Toogood says of the London scene at the time. “I was treading a path in between, and that’s where I’ve stayed. It’s only been in the last couple of years that the spotlight has been put on the area that I’ve been working in.”

Toogood’s first interiors gigs were for Comme des Garçons and Dover Street Market – it was then that she realised she’d need to make her own objects to truly realise her vision. Her furniture practice was born, and clothing came a few years later in partnership with her sister, Erica, who worked as a pattern cutter. What started as a collaborative project has since grown into a fully fledged wing of the Toogood design universe: a whimsical and playful, but ultimately utilitarian and humanist house that refuses to choose between form and function.

While all these categories – furniture, homeware, interiors and clothing – may strike others as unwieldy for a Camden-based team of just over 25, Toogood dwells less on categorical conventions and more on the rules that can be bent and broken, particularly from a sculptural perspective. “Silhouette is something I’m always challenging: why does a jacket, or a chair, have to look or be shaped like that? I’m well aware that we have a lot of stuff in our lives, and if we’re going to contribute, it needs to be meaningful and connecting. There needs to be a point of difference, or there’s no point,” she says.

This pursuit has, on several occasions, put Toogood commercially too far ahead of the curve – a position she’s not unfamiliar with. The first time she showcased the brand’s now-bestselling Baker trousers, industry buyers called the style unsellable. That was 13 years ago; now, voluminous wide-legged cropped trousers proliferate in mainstream boutiques. There’s also her signature Roly Poly chair, with its generous curves and squat, inviting seat.

“The first three years, nobody looked at it,” Toogood recalls of her most famous design. “I didn’t sell anything, and I sold the license to [Italian furniture company] Driade … which is now doing really well with it. It can be frustrating, but that’s OK – that’s my role. I accept that maybe I’m more of the aggregator and catalyst for change, and I’d rather be up front.”

Whether she’s sketching in the studio or mulling over an incoming collaboration (previous partners include Birkenstock, Carhartt Wip, Stockholm-based furniture player Hem, and eyewear label Cubitts), Toogood’s process is shaped more by intuition, emotion and sensitivity than technicalities and credentials. Indeed, she calls The World of Interiors – not her fine art and history of art degree – her “main education”, and the reason she knows to look at spaces with an eye on how lives are lived, rather than how houses and rooms fit into a look or trend.

Unlike other studios, Toogood’s team doesn’t design for a client profile of collaged characteristics; nor do she and Erica make clothes by manipulating existing patterns. She eschews social media and instead prioritises meditating and spending time outside (she grew up in the countryside “in the middle of nowhere”, so a need for green space is perhaps ingrained). She abstains from the algorithm, with its turbo-speed trend cycles, completely. Yes, an analogue process makes the work harder and more time-consuming, but she and her team feel more connected to the end product, and their own creative instincts, as a result.

“I think the role of the designer previously would have been, ‘How can I give you the most functional object or how can I resolve all the problems to give you the most ergonomic chair?’ I’m thinking about it more like a mother would think: ‘What is it you need? Do you need a hug right now, or do you need to go off and have fun?’”

I tell her the adjectives peppered through our conversation – emotional, sensitive, empathetic, maternal – are striking for how often they’re framed as negatives in a corporate setting. She raises AI, and how what makes us human – from creativity and innovation to the imperfection of the handmade – is only becoming more precious: “All of those words have been previously connected to gender, and I think it’s interesting now that they are very much connected to the future of what it is to be a human.”

This will bring Toogood’s existing fans some comfort: the brand’s love of humble, tactile materials and the embrace of play (the latest clothing collection was inspired by, and features doodles from, Toogood’s young children) won’t be stripped away, even as the business slowly, organically grows. (You won’t find a Toogood NFT, or heaven forbid, a Baker trouser-wearing Labubu, any time soon.)

But there are projects the designer has yet to tick off her bucket list – the first being a hotel, which would make full use of her multidisciplinary inclinations. The second? A sculptural playground à la Isamu Noguchi, where toddlers and adults alike can let their creative juices flow.

“I think play is really important, and something that’s getting diminished for children and adults. I’m really proud of the fact that I’m very much in contact with my creativity, and I’d like to help others be in contact with their creativity,” she continues. “We are expecting ourselves to behave and react like machines, and we’re not. Dancing, making music, reading, drawing, sculpting, gardening – all of these things are essential.”
